text: Vigilance — a demanding form of sustained attention — decays in a remarkably consistent pattern. In 1948, psychologist Norman Mackworth placed radar operators in a task requiring them to detect rare signal blips over a two-hour watch. Performance dropped sharply in the first thirty minutes and plateaued at a degraded level thereafter. This "Mackworth decrement" has since been replicated across dozens of tasks and cultures. The mechanism appears to involve depletion of phasic norepinephrine responses: the brain stops generating a sharp neural alert to rare but meaningful events, blurring signal against noise. Modern knowledge workers face their own invisible radar screen — the inbox, the feed — and suffer the same decay.
question: According to the passage, what is the neural mechanism behind the Mackworth vigilance decrement?

options.text: Excess dopamine release suppresses the anterior cingulate's conflict monitoring.
Phasic norepinephrine responses are depleted, blurring signal against noise.
The prefrontal cortex exhausts its glucose supply and enters conservation mode.
Serotonin binds to locus coeruleus receptors, slowing arousal recovery.
